summary: Jeff Atwood shares a Visual Studio 2005 macro he created to enumerate all code snippets installed on a system, building on Francesco Balena's console application. The macro reads from the Windows registry to find both system-wide and per-user snippet paths, then generates an HTML file listing all snippets with their shortcut text. The post provides step-by-step instructions for installing and running the macro, noting it supports multiple languages including C#, VB, J#, and XML.
key_points: The snippet manager stores all its configuration in the Windows registry, which the macro reads to find snippet paths
The macro generates an HTML report of all snippets and opens it directly in the Visual Studio IDE
The tool supports enumerating snippets for multiple languages by changing a single variable
This continues Atwood's pattern of creating developer productivity tools as IDE macros
money_quotes: I found out the hard way that the snippet manager writes all of its changes to the registry.
Does anyone actually *use* J#? C'mon. Seriously.
key_insight: Developer productivity can be improved by building small tools that surface hidden IDE capabilities, like enumerating all available code snippets from the registry.
